nativeRange.habitat: Found along coral reef drop-offs near staghorn and fire corals and rubble mounds, at depths between 2 and 15 metres.

diet.notes: Primarily a macroalgae grazer, this species should be offered a varied vegetarian diet including dried seaweed sheets (nori), fresh vegetables, herbivore-formulated pellets and flakes, and occasional meaty supplements such as Spirulina-enriched brine shrimp and Mysis shrimp. Providing generous daily algae offerings is essential to keeping this species reliably reef-safe.

avoidWith: very aggressive tankmates that may stress this generally peaceful species

summary: The Tongan Black Foxface is a rare rabbitfish endemic to Tonga, recognized by its near-black body, yellow pectoral fins, and a white patterned stripe across the gill cover. Generally peaceful and an active algae grazer, it carries venomous fin spines and is considered reef-safe when kept well fed.
careNotes: The Tongan Black Foxface is a rare rabbitfish endemic to the Vava'u island group in northern Tonga. The body is nearly uniformly black, set off by yellow pectoral fins, a yellow posterior margin to the dorsal, anal, and caudal fins, and a white lace-patterned stripe across the gill cover (the operculum) — a marking that distinguishes it from the related Fiji Foxface. Like all foxfaces, it is generally peaceful with non-conspecific tankmates but should not be housed with other rabbitfish unless the system is very large and a bonded pair has been established. IMPORTANT: All dorsal and anal fin spines are venomous and deliver a painful sting comparable in mechanism to stonefish venom. Handle only with nets or aquarium tools, never bare-handed. The species displays a mottled "fright" colouration when stressed, sleeping, or freshly introduced — this is normal and not a sign of illness. Reef safety is conditional on a well-fed specimen: hungry individuals have been reported to nip at LPS (large polyp stony) and soft corals, so generous daily macroalgae offerings such as nori clips and seaweed sheets are essential. Adults are reported to live in pairs in the wild while juveniles form small schools; in captivity most aquarists keep a single specimen, though a bonded pair can succeed in a large system. The species is listed as Vulnerable on the IUCN Red List due to its small endemic range and collection pressure from both the food and aquarium trades, so sourcing from reputable suppliers is worth considering. Water parameters and care broadly mirror those of the more familiar Foxface Lo (Siganus vulpinus).
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented. Like other rabbitfishes the species is presumed to be a pelagic broadcast spawner cued by lunar and tidal cycles, making replication in home aquaria essentially infeasible. Wild adults are reported to occur in pairs; whether this represents true monogamous pair-bonding or transient pairing during the spawning season is not well established.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source: 125 gal minimum tank, care level Moderate, peaceful temperament except with other rabbitfish, reef safe with caution, SG 1.020-1.025, pH 8.1-8.4, temperature 72-78 F (22-25.6 C), dKH 8-12, max size 9 in. Collection landing page (https://www.liveaquaria.com/collections/foxface-rabbitfish) returned minimal data; product-specific page used instead.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Siganidae, order Acanthuriformes), max length (22.0 cm TL), trophic level 2.0 (herbivore on seaweeds), depth range (2-15 m), endemic distribution (Tonga, Vava'u), and IUCN Vulnerable status. Confirms LiveAquaria's 9 in / ~22.9 cm figure (22 cm on FishBase).

additional.name: Wikipedia - Black foxface
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy, max length (22 cm), distribution (endemic to Tonga, Vava'u), depth (2-15 m), distinctive coloration (yellow pectorals, vermiculated white opercular stripe), social structure (adults in pairs, juveniles in schools), and venomous spines (compared to stonefish venom). Confirms IUCN Vulnerable status.

additional.name: Reef Builders - Black Foxface Rabbitfish
additional.notes: Hobbyist trade-introduction source. Confirmed distinguishing features vs Fiji Foxface, fright colouration behavior, rarity in the aquarium trade, and macroalgae grazing role.

fish.breedingNotes: Like other rabbitfishes, the Tongan Black Foxface is presumed to be a pelagic broadcast spawner whose reproductive activity is cued by lunar and tidal cycles. No captive breeding has been documented.
